title: Scan chain hold violation after CTS
description:
# Scan chain hold violation

Post-CTS (clock tree synthesis), a hold violation appeared on the scan chain between two adjacent flops `scan_reg_a` and `scan_reg_b`. Slack is -60 ps. The design met hold in pre-CTS ideal clocks.

There's no combinational logic between the two flops — just a short scan wire. Clock period is 2.0 ns, hold check is at cycle 0. Uncertainty 40 ps.

**Your job:** figure out why a short combinational path suddenly can't meet hold, and pick a fix.

reportedViolation.summary: hold VIOLATED (-0.060 ns) on scan_reg_a/Q -> scan_reg_b/D

rootCauseOptions.label: Clock skew: scan_reg_b's clock path has one more buffer than scan_reg_a, so its capture edge arrives later — the short data path reaches D before hold is guaranteed
The combinational data path is too long for setup
Scan flops are too weak to drive the next stage

acceptedFixes.description: Insert a delay buffer on n_scan_q between scan_reg_a and scan_reg_b
Rebalance clock tree: remove one buffer from scan_reg_b branch to equalize insertion delay

hintTree.nudge: Pre-CTS it met hold. Post-CTS it doesn't. What changed between those two timing runs?
hintTree.hint: Hold is violated when data arrives before the capture edge is ready. Check the clock network — specifically the insertion delay at each flop's clock pin.
hintTree.walkthrough: Run `report_clock_network -clock clk`. scan_reg_a has insertion delay ~0.14 ns (clk → buf1 → buf2a → a); scan_reg_b has ~0.195 ns (clk → buf1 → buf2b → buf3b → b). That 55 ps skew plus the 18 ps wire on n_scan_q means data arrives before capture can latch reliably. Fix: insert a delay buffer on the data path, or rebalance the clock tree.

steps.title: Read the hold timing report
steps.goal: Run a min-delay (hold) timing report on the failing path. Understand what hold slack means.

checkpoint.question: In a hold check, what goes wrong when slack is negative?

options.label: Data arrives at the capture flop before its hold window closes — new data corrupts the previous cycle's capture
Data arrives too late for the next rising edge

steps.insight: Hold violations mean data changes too fast — it reaches the capture flop before that flop has finished latching the previous value. This is a race condition between the data path and the clock path.

steps.title: Check the data path delay
steps.goal: The scan wire between these two flops is very short. How much total data path delay is there?

checkpoint.question: How long is the combinational data path (CK-to-Q + wire)?

steps.insight: The total data delay is tiny — only ~113 ps. With no combinational logic between the flops, data races through almost instantly. This is a classic scan-chain hold setup.

steps.title: Investigate the clock network
steps.goal: This met hold with ideal clocks but fails post-CTS. The only thing CTS changed is clock arrival times. Compare insertion delays.

checkpoint.question: What's different about the clock paths to these two flops?

options.label: scan_reg_b has one extra buffer (clk_buf_3b) — its clock arrives ~55 ps later
Both flops have identical clock paths

steps.insight: scan_reg_a: clk → buf1 → buf2a → CK (~140 ps). scan_reg_b: clk → buf1 → buf2b → buf3b → CK (~195 ps). That extra buffer gives scan_reg_b 55 ps more insertion delay — its hold window starts later, but the data already arrived.

steps.title: Connect the dots
steps.goal: Combine what you know: fast data path + clock skew at capture = hold problem. What's the root cause?

options.label: Clock skew: capture clock arrives 55 ps late due to an extra CTS buffer, but the 113 ps data path is too short to absorb it
The scan flops are too weak

steps.insight: Classic post-CTS hold violation. The fix is either slow down the data (insert a delay buffer) or speed up the capture clock (rebalance CTS). In practice, tools insert hold-fix buffers automatically — but you need to understand *why* to debug when they don't.
